draft.introduction: Revelation 5:1–14 introduces the sealed scroll that no one in heaven or earth can open—except the Lamb who was slain. The vision redefines divine power as sacrificial love. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: And one of the elders said to me, “Weep no more; behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll and its seven seals.” And between the throne and the four living creatures and among the elders I saw a Lamb standing, as though it had been slain.
translation.original_text: και εις εκ των πρεσβυτερων λεγει μοι μη κλαιε

translation.text: And they sang a new song, saying, “Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation, and you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth.”
translation.original_text: και αδουσιν ωδην καινην λεγοντες αξιος ει

translator_notes.note: The dramatic reversal—John is told to look for a Lion but sees a Lamb—is the hermeneutical key to the entire Apocalypse. Power is redefined through sacrifice. The Lamb bears the marks of slaughter yet stands, conquering through self-giving rather than violence.
